<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Tokyo Medical School]][[ja:東京医学校]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11524573]] Wikidata name: Tokyo Medical School [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:00, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Tokyo Medical School''' (東京医学校) was an early Meiji-era government medical education institution located in Tokyo Prefecture. It was one of the "specialized schools" of its time, representing higher education distinct from later vocational schools under the {{ill|Senmon Gakkō Rei|ja|専門学校令|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senmon Gakkō Rei|1=Senmon Gakkō Rei|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3258590}}. [[File:Koishikawa Annex.jpg|thumb|right|200px|The former Tokyo Medical School main building, relocated to the Koishikawa Botanical Garden, now the Koishikawa Annex of the University Museum.]]
== Overview == In May 1868 (Keiō 4), the {{ill|Meiji government|ja|明治政府|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji government|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 明治維新|1=Meiji restoration|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8707}} took over the {{ill|Medical School|ja|医学所|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Medical School|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|1=Medical School|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10950500}} of the former {{ill|Tokugawa shogunate|1=Tokugawa shogunate|2=ja|3=江戸幕府|ja|Tokugawa shogunate|1=Tokugawa shogunate|2=ja|3=江戸幕府|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa shogunate|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and reestablished it as the "Medical School." It later became part of the {{ill|University East Campus|ja|大学東校|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/University East Campus|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大学校 (1869年)|1=Daigakkō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11434269}} and was renamed Tokyo Medical School in 1874.
Together with the {{ill|Kaisei School|ja|開成学校|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaisei School|qq=}}, Tokyo Medical School formed the foundation of the {{ill|(Old) Tokyo University|ja|東京大学 (1877-1886)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/(Old) Tokyo University|1=Tokyo University|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11524659}} established in 1877. It is the predecessor of the [[:en:Graduate School of Medicine and Faculty of Medicine, University of Tokyo|University of Tokyo Graduate School of Medicine and Faculty of Medicine]].
== History ==
=== As the "Medical School" === In November 1861 (Bunkyū 1), the Tokugawa shogunate established the Western-style {{ill|Medical School|ja|医学所|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Medical School|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|1=Medical School|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10950500}}. In May 1868 (Keiō 4), the Meiji government took over this institution and renamed it the "Medical School" on {{ill|June 26 (old calendar)|ja|6月26日 (旧暦)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/June 26 (old calendar)|1=26th day of the 6th month in the Chinese calendar|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q839738}} (August 14, 1868, Gregorian calendar). In January 1869, William Willis, a physician attached to the British legation, began teaching there.
In February 1869, the Medical School merged with the "Large Hospital" to form the "Medical School and Hospital." On {{ill|July 8 (old calendar)|ja|7月8日 (旧暦)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/July 8 (old calendar)|1=8th day of the 7th month in the Chinese calendar|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q839706}} (August 15, 1869), it became part of the [[:en:Daigakkō|University]], under its jurisdiction as a "branch."
=== As "University East Campus" === {{Main|University East Campus}} On December 17, 1869 (January 18, 1870), the Medical School was renamed the "University East Campus" as part of the University restructuring. However, internal conflicts led to the closure of the {{ill|Main Campus|ja|大学本校|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Main Campus|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大学校 (1869年)|1=Daigakkō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11434269}} in August 1870, effectively rendering the University non-functional. The East Campus then operated independently, with new regulations implemented by October.
On {{ill|July 18 (old calendar)|ja|7月18日 (旧暦)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/July 18 (old calendar)|1=18th day of the 7th month in the Chinese calendar|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q839924}} (September 2, 1871), following the formal abolition of the University and the establishment of the [[:en:Ministry of Education, Science, Sports and Culture|Ministry of Education]], the East Campus was renamed "Tōkō" on {{ill|July 21 (old calendar)|ja|7月21日 (旧暦)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/July 21 (old calendar)|1=21st day of the 7th month in the Chinese calendar|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q839764}} (September 5, 1871). During this period, the transition from British to German medical education began.
=== As Tokyo Medical School === Following the introduction of the {{ill|Education System Order|ja|学制|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Education System Order|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Education System Order|WD=Q11448123}} in 1872, the school was renamed the "First University District Medical School" on {{ill|August 3 (old calendar)|ja|8月3日 (旧暦)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/August 3 (old calendar)|1=3rd day of the 8th month in the Chinese calendar|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q840073}} (September 5, 1872). In 1874, it was renamed Tokyo Medical School.
The school offered a two-year preparatory course and a five-year main course, primarily using German as the medium of instruction. In 1874, it introduced a simplified pharmaceutical training program (two years) and a short medical training course (three years), both taught in Japanese.
In 1877, Tokyo Medical School merged with the {{ill|Kaisei School|ja|東京開成学校|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaisei School|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 開成学校}} to form {{ill|(Old) Tokyo University|ja|東京大学 (1877-1886)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/(Old) Tokyo University|1=Tokyo University|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11524659}}. Initially, the Medical School maintained significant independence within the university, including its own head, equivalent to the university president.
=== Transition to German Medicine === Early in the Meiji era, the government favored British medicine due to its ties with Britain and its clinical focus. However, advocates like [[:en:Sagara Tomoyasu|Sagara Chian]] and {{ill|Iwasa Jun|ja|岩佐純|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwasa Jun|1=Iwasa Jun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q26243551}} promoted German medicine for its university-based research emphasis. Influential figures such as [[:en:Guido Verbeck|Guido Verbeck]] supported this shift, leading to the adoption of German medical education in 1871.
== Campus Locations and Legacy == The school initially occupied the former Medical School site in [[:en:Ueno|Ueno]] but later moved to {{ill|Hongo|ja|本郷|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hongo|1=Hongo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q19927172}}. Its 1876 campus, built in pseudo-Western architectural style, survived the {{ill|Great Kanto Earthquake|ja|関東大震災|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Great Kanto Earthquake|1=aftermath of 1923 Great Kantō earthquake|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3742341}} and was relocated to the {{ill|Koishikawa Botanical Garden|ja|小石川植物園|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Koishikawa Botanical Garden|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 東京大学大学院理学系研究科附属植物園|1=Koishikawa Botanical Garden|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1064892}}. It now serves as the oldest building associated with the University of Tokyo.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Tokyo Medical School]][[ja:東京医学校]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11524573]] Wikidata name: Tokyo Medical School [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:00, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Tokyo Medical School''' (東京医学校) was an early Meiji-era government medical education institution located in Tokyo Prefecture. It was one of the "specialized schools" of its time, representing higher education distinct from later vocational schools under the {{ill|Senmon Gakkō Rei|ja|専門学校令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senmon Gakkō Rei|1=Senmon Gakkō Rei|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3258590}}. [[File:Koishikawa Annex.jpg|thumb|right|200px|The former Tokyo Medical School main building, relocated to the Koishikawa Botanical Garden, now the Koishikawa Annex of the University Museum.]]
== Overview == In May 1868 (Keiō 4), the {{ill|Meiji government|ja|明治政府|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji government|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 明治維新|1=Meiji restoration|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8707}} took over the {{ill|Medical School|ja|医学所|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Medical School|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|1=Medical School|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10950500}} of the former {{ill|Tokugawa shogunate|1=Tokugawa shogunate|2=ja|3=江戸幕府|ja|Tokugawa shogunate|1=Tokugawa shogunate|2=ja|3=江戸幕府|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa shogunate|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and reestablished it as the "Medical School." It later became part of the {{ill|University East Campus|ja|大学東校|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/University East Campus|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大学校 (1869年)|1=Daigakkō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11434269}} and was renamed Tokyo Medical School in 1874.
Together with the {{ill|Kaisei School|ja|開成学校|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaisei School|qq=}}, Tokyo Medical School formed the foundation of the {{ill|(Old) Tokyo University|ja|東京大学 (1877-1886)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/(Old) Tokyo University|1=Tokyo University|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11524659}} established in 1877. It is the predecessor of the [[:en:Graduate School of Medicine and Faculty of Medicine, University of Tokyo|University of Tokyo Graduate School of Medicine and Faculty of Medicine]].
== History ==
=== As the "Medical School" === In November 1861 (Bunkyū 1), the Tokugawa shogunate established the Western-style {{ill|Medical School|ja|医学所|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Medical School|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|1=Medical School|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10950500}}. In May 1868 (Keiō 4), the Meiji government took over this institution and renamed it the "Medical School" on {{ill|June 26 (old calendar)|ja|6月26日 (旧暦)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/June 26 (old calendar)|1=26th day of the 6th month in the Chinese calendar|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q839738}} (August 14, 1868, Gregorian calendar). In January 1869, William Willis, a physician attached to the British legation, began teaching there.
In February 1869, the Medical School merged with the "Large Hospital" to form the "Medical School and Hospital." On {{ill|July 8 (old calendar)|ja|7月8日 (旧暦)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/July 8 (old calendar)|1=8th day of the 7th month in the Chinese calendar|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q839706}} (August 15, 1869), it became part of the [[:en:Daigakkō|University]], under its jurisdiction as a "branch."
=== As "University East Campus" === {{Main|University East Campus}} On December 17, 1869 (January 18, 1870), the Medical School was renamed the "University East Campus" as part of the University restructuring. However, internal conflicts led to the closure of the {{ill|Main Campus|ja|大学本校|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Main Campus|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大学校 (1869年)|1=Daigakkō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11434269}} in August 1870, effectively rendering the University non-functional. The East Campus then operated independently, with new regulations implemented by October.
On {{ill|July 18 (old calendar)|ja|7月18日 (旧暦)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/July 18 (old calendar)|1=18th day of the 7th month in the Chinese calendar|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q839924}} (September 2, 1871), following the formal abolition of the University and the establishment of the [[:en:Ministry of Education, Science, Sports and Culture|Ministry of Education]], the East Campus was renamed "Tōkō" on {{ill|July 21 (old calendar)|ja|7月21日 (旧暦)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/July 21 (old calendar)|1=21st day of the 7th month in the Chinese calendar|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q839764}} (September 5, 1871). During this period, the transition from British to German medical education began.
=== As Tokyo Medical School === Following the introduction of the {{ill|Education System Order|ja|学制|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Education System Order|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Education System Order|WD=Q11448123}} in 1872, the school was renamed the "First University District Medical School" on {{ill|August 3 (old calendar)|ja|8月3日 (旧暦)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/August 3 (old calendar)|1=3rd day of the 8th month in the Chinese calendar|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q840073}} (September 5, 1872). In 1874, it was renamed Tokyo Medical School.
The school offered a two-year preparatory course and a five-year main course, primarily using German as the medium of instruction. In 1874, it introduced a simplified pharmaceutical training program (two years) and a short medical training course (three years), both taught in Japanese.
In 1877, Tokyo Medical School merged with the {{ill|Kaisei School|ja|東京開成学校|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaisei School|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 開成学校}} to form {{ill|(Old) Tokyo University|ja|東京大学 (1877-1886)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/(Old) Tokyo University|1=Tokyo University|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11524659}}. Initially, the Medical School maintained significant independence within the university, including its own head, equivalent to the university president.
=== Transition to German Medicine === Early in the Meiji era, the government favored British medicine due to its ties with Britain and its clinical focus. However, advocates like [[:en:Sagara Tomoyasu|Sagara Chian]] and {{ill|Iwasa Jun|ja|岩佐純|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwasa Jun|1=Iwasa Jun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q26243551}} promoted German medicine for its university-based research emphasis. Influential figures such as [[:en:Guido Verbeck|Guido Verbeck]] supported this shift, leading to the adoption of German medical education in 1871.
== Campus Locations and Legacy == The school initially occupied the former Medical School site in [[:en:Ueno|Ueno]] but later moved to {{ill|Hongo|ja|本郷|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hongo|1=Hongo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q19927172}}. Its 1876 campus, built in pseudo-Western architectural style, survived the {{ill|Great Kanto Earthquake|ja|関東大震災|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Great Kanto Earthquake|1=aftermath of 1923 Great Kantō earthquake|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3742341}} and was relocated to the {{ill|Koishikawa Botanical Garden|ja|小石川植物園|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Koishikawa Botanical Garden|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 東京大学大学院理学系研究科附属植物園|1=Koishikawa Botanical Garden|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1064892}}. It now serves as the oldest building associated with the University of Tokyo.
== Related Literature == ; Books * Akira Kajita, ''History of Medicine'', Kodansha Academic Library, 2003 ISBN 4061596144 * Masao Terasaki, ''History of the University of Tokyo: Pioneer of the University System'', Kodansha Academic Library, 2007 ISBN 9784061597990
== Related Articles == * [[:en:Erwin Bälz|Erwin von Baelz]] * {{ill|Keio Medical School|ja|慶應義塾医学所|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Keio Medical School|1=Keio Medical School|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11495139}} * {{ill|Nagasaki Medical School|ja|長崎医学校|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagasaki Medical School|1=Nagasaki Medical School|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11652415}}
{{Tokyo University Predecessors}} {{school-stub}}

However, advocates like [[:en:Sagara Tomoyasu|Sagara Chian]] and {{ill|Iwasa Jun|ja|岩佐純|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwasa Jun|1=Iwasa Jun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q26243551}} promoted German medicine for its university-based research emphasis. Influential figures such as [[:en:Guido Verbeck|Guido Verbeck]] supported this shift, leading to the adoption of German medical education in 1871.
== Campus Locations and Legacy == The school initially occupied the former Medical School site in [[:en:Ueno|Ueno]] but later moved to {{ill|Hongo|ja|本郷|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hongo|1=Hongo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q19927172}}. Its 1876 campus, built in pseudo-Western architectural style, survived the {{ill|Great Kanto Earthquake|ja|関東大震災|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Great Kanto Earthquake|1=aftermath of 1923 Great Kantō earthquake|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3742341}} and was relocated to the {{ill|Koishikawa Botanical Garden|ja|小石川植物園|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Koishikawa Botanical Garden|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 東京大学大学院理学系研究科附属植物園|1=Koishikawa Botanical Garden|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1064892}}. It now serves as the oldest building associated with the University of Tokyo.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Tokyo Medical School]][[ja:東京医学校]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11524573]] Wikidata name: Tokyo Medical School [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:00, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Tokyo Medical School''' (東京医学校) was an early Meiji-era government medical education institution located in Tokyo Prefecture. It was one of the "specialized schools" of its time, representing higher education distinct from later vocational schools under the {{ill|Senmon Gakkō Rei|ja|専門学校令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senmon Gakkō Rei|1=Senmon Gakkō Rei|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3258590}}. [[File:Koishikawa Annex.jpg|thumb|right|200px|The former Tokyo Medical School main building, relocated to the Koishikawa Botanical Garden, now the Koishikawa Annex of the University Museum.]]
== Overview == In May 1868 (Keiō 4), the {{ill|Meiji government|ja|明治政府|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji government|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 明治維新|1=Meiji restoration|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8707}} took over the {{ill|Medical School|ja|医学所|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Medical School|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|1=Medical School|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10950500}} of the former {{ill|Tokugawa shogunate|1=Tokugawa shogunate|2=ja|3=江戸幕府|ja|Tokugawa shogunate|1=Tokugawa shogunate|2=ja|3=江戸幕府|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa shogunate|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and reestablished it as the "Medical School." It later became part of the {{ill|University East Campus|ja|大学東校|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/University East Campus|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大学校 (1869年)|1=Daigakkō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11434269}} and was renamed Tokyo Medical School in 1874.
Together with the {{ill|Kaisei School|ja|開成学校|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaisei School|qq=}}, Tokyo Medical School formed the foundation of the {{ill|(Old) Tokyo University|ja|東京大学 (1877-1886)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/(Old) Tokyo University|1=Tokyo University|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11524659}} established in 1877. It is the predecessor of the [[:en:Graduate School of Medicine and Faculty of Medicine, University of Tokyo|University of Tokyo Graduate School of Medicine and Faculty of Medicine]].
== History ==
=== As the "Medical School" === In November 1861 (Bunkyū 1), the Tokugawa shogunate established the Western-style {{ill|Medical School|ja|医学所|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Medical School|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|1=Medical School|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10950500}}. In May 1868 (Keiō 4), the Meiji government took over this institution and renamed it the "Medical School" on {{ill|June 26 (old calendar)|ja|6月26日 (旧暦)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/June 26 (old calendar)|1=26th day of the 6th month in the Chinese calendar|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q839738}} (August 14, 1868, Gregorian calendar). In January 1869, William Willis, a physician attached to the British legation, began teaching there.
In February 1869, the Medical School merged with the "Large Hospital" to form the "Medical School and Hospital." On {{ill|July 8 (old calendar)|ja|7月8日 (旧暦)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/July 8 (old calendar)|1=8th day of the 7th month in the Chinese calendar|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q839706}} (August 15, 1869), it became part of the [[:en:Daigakkō|University]], under its jurisdiction as a "branch."
=== As "University East Campus" === {{Main|University East Campus}} On December 17, 1869 (January 18, 1870), the Medical School was renamed the "University East Campus" as part of the University restructuring. However, internal conflicts led to the closure of the {{ill|Main Campus|ja|大学本校|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Main Campus|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大学校 (1869年)|1=Daigakkō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11434269}} in August 1870, effectively rendering the University non-functional. The East Campus then operated independently, with new regulations implemented by October.
On {{ill|July 18 (old calendar)|ja|7月18日 (旧暦)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/July 18 (old calendar)|1=18th day of the 7th month in the Chinese calendar|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q839924}} (September 2, 1871), following the formal abolition of the University and the establishment of the [[:en:Ministry of Education, Science, Sports and Culture|Ministry of Education]], the East Campus was renamed "Tōkō" on {{ill|July 21 (old calendar)|ja|7月21日 (旧暦)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/July 21 (old calendar)|1=21st day of the 7th month in the Chinese calendar|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q839764}} (September 5, 1871). During this period, the transition from British to German medical education began.
=== As Tokyo Medical School === Following the introduction of the {{ill|Education System Order|ja|学制|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Education System Order|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Education System Order|WD=Q11448123}} in 1872, the school was renamed the "First University District Medical School" on {{ill|August 3 (old calendar)|ja|8月3日 (旧暦)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/August 3 (old calendar)|1=3rd day of the 8th month in the Chinese calendar|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q840073}} (September 5, 1872). In 1874, it was renamed Tokyo Medical School.
The school offered a two-year preparatory course and a five-year main course, primarily using German as the medium of instruction. In 1874, it introduced a simplified pharmaceutical training program (two years) and a short medical training course (three years), both taught in Japanese.
In 1877, Tokyo Medical School merged with the {{ill|Kaisei School|ja|東京開成学校|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaisei School|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 開成学校}} to form {{ill|(Old) Tokyo University|ja|東京大学 (1877-1886)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/(Old) Tokyo University|1=Tokyo University|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11524659}}. Initially, the Medical School maintained significant independence within the university, including its own head, equivalent to the university president.
=== Transition to German Medicine === Early in the Meiji era, the government favored British medicine due to its ties with Britain and its clinical focus. However, advocates like [[:en:Sagara Tomoyasu|Sagara Chian]] and {{ill|Iwasa Jun|ja|岩佐純|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwasa Jun|1=Iwasa Jun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q26243551}} promoted German medicine for its university-based research emphasis. Influential figures such as [[:en:Guido Verbeck|Guido Verbeck]] supported this shift, leading to the adoption of German medical education in 1871.
== Campus Locations and Legacy == The school initially occupied the former Medical School site in [[:en:Ueno|Ueno]] but later moved to {{ill|Hongo|ja|本郷|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hongo|1=Hongo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q19927172}}. Its 1876 campus, built in pseudo-Western architectural style, survived the {{ill|Great Kanto Earthquake|ja|関東大震災|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Great Kanto Earthquake|1=aftermath of 1923 Great Kantō earthquake|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3742341}} and was relocated to the {{ill|Koishikawa Botanical Garden|ja|小石川植物園|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Koishikawa Botanical Garden|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 東京大学大学院理学系研究科附属植物園|1=Koishikawa Botanical Garden|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1064892}}. It now serves as the oldest building associated with the University of Tokyo.

On {{ill|July 8 (old calendar)|ja|7月8日 (旧暦)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/July 8 (old calendar)|1=8th day of the 7th month in the Chinese calendar|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q839706}} (August 15, 1869), it became part of the [[:en:Daigakkō|University]], under its jurisdiction as a "branch."
=== As "University East Campus" === {{Main|University East Campus}} On December 17, 1869 (January 18, 1870), the Medical School was renamed the "University East Campus" as part of the University restructuring. However, internal conflicts led to the closure of the {{ill|Main Campus|ja|大学本校|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Main Campus|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大学校 (1869年)|1=Daigakkō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11434269}} in August 1870, effectively rendering the University non-functional. The East Campus then operated independently, with new regulations implemented by October.
On {{ill|July 18 (old calendar)|ja|7月18日 (旧暦)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/July 18 (old calendar)|1=18th day of the 7th month in the Chinese calendar|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q839924}} (September 2, 1871), following the formal abolition of the University and the establishment of the [[:en:Ministry of Education, Science, Sports and Culture|Ministry of Education]], the East Campus was renamed "Tōkō" on {{ill|July 21 (old calendar)|ja|7月21日 (旧暦)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/July 21 (old calendar)|1=21st day of the 7th month in the Chinese calendar|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q839764}} (September 5, 1871). During this period, the transition from British to German medical education began.
=== As Tokyo Medical School === Following the introduction of the {{ill|Education System Order|ja|学制|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Education System Order|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Education System Order|WD=Q11448123}} in 1872, the school was renamed the "First University District Medical School" on {{ill|August 3 (old calendar)|ja|8月3日 (旧暦)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/August 3 (old calendar)|1=3rd day of the 8th month in the Chinese calendar|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q840073}} (September 5, 1872). In 1874, it was renamed Tokyo Medical School.
The school offered a two-year preparatory course and a five-year main course, primarily using German as the medium of instruction. In 1874, it introduced a simplified pharmaceutical training program (two years) and a short medical training course (three years), both taught in Japanese.
In 1877, Tokyo Medical School merged with the {{ill|Kaisei School|ja|東京開成学校|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaisei School|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 開成学校}} to form {{ill|(Old) Tokyo University|ja|東京大学 (1877-1886)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/(Old) Tokyo University|1=Tokyo University|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11524659}}. Initially, the Medical School maintained significant independence within the university, including its own head, equivalent to the university president.
=== Transition to German Medicine === Early in the Meiji era, the government favored British medicine due to its ties with Britain and its clinical focus. However, advocates like [[:en:Sagara Tomoyasu|Sagara Chian]] and {{ill|Iwasa Jun|ja|岩佐純|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwasa Jun|1=Iwasa Jun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q26243551}} promoted German medicine for its university-based research emphasis. Influential figures such as [[:en:Guido Verbeck|Guido Verbeck]] supported this shift, leading to the adoption of German medical education in 1871.
== Campus Locations and Legacy == The school initially occupied the former Medical School site in [[:en:Ueno|Ueno]] but later moved to {{ill|Hongo|ja|本郷|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hongo|1=Hongo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q19927172}}. Its 1876 campus, built in pseudo-Western architectural style, survived the {{ill|Great Kanto Earthquake|ja|関東大震災|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Great Kanto Earthquake|1=aftermath of 1923 Great Kantō earthquake|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3742341}} and was relocated to the {{ill|Koishikawa Botanical Garden|ja|小石川植物園|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Koishikawa Botanical Garden|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 東京大学大学院理学系研究科附属植物園|1=Koishikawa Botanical Garden|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1064892}}. It now serves as the oldest building associated with the University of Tokyo.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Tokyo Medical School]][[ja:東京医学校]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11524573]] Wikidata name: Tokyo Medical School [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:00, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Tokyo Medical School''' (東京医学校) was an early Meiji-era government medical education institution located in Tokyo Prefecture. It was one of the "specialized schools" of its time, representing higher education distinct from later vocational schools under the {{ill|Senmon Gakkō Rei|ja|専門学校令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senmon Gakkō Rei|1=Senmon Gakkō Rei|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3258590}}. [[File:Koishikawa Annex.jpg|thumb|right|200px|The former Tokyo Medical School main building, relocated to the Koishikawa Botanical Garden, now the Koishikawa Annex of the University Museum.]]
== Overview == In May 1868 (Keiō 4), the {{ill|Meiji government|ja|明治政府|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji government|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 明治維新|1=Meiji restoration|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8707}} took over the {{ill|Medical School|ja|医学所|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Medical School|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|1=Medical School|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10950500}} of the former {{ill|Tokugawa shogunate|1=Tokugawa shogunate|2=ja|3=江戸幕府|ja|Tokugawa shogunate|1=Tokugawa shogunate|2=ja|3=江戸幕府|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa shogunate|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and reestablished it as the "Medical School." It later became part of the {{ill|University East Campus|ja|大学東校|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/University East Campus|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大学校 (1869年)|1=Daigakkō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11434269}} and was renamed Tokyo Medical School in 1874.
Together with the {{ill|Kaisei School|ja|開成学校|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaisei School|qq=}}, Tokyo Medical School formed the foundation of the {{ill|(Old) Tokyo University|ja|東京大学 (1877-1886)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/(Old) Tokyo University|1=Tokyo University|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11524659}} established in 1877. It is the predecessor of the [[:en:Graduate School of Medicine and Faculty of Medicine, University of Tokyo|University of Tokyo Graduate School of Medicine and Faculty of Medicine]].

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Tokyo Medical School]][[ja:東京医学校]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11524573]] Wikidata name: Tokyo Medical School [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:00, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Tokyo Medical School''' (東京医学校) was an early Meiji-era government medical education institution located in Tokyo Prefecture. It was one of the "specialized schools" of its time, representing higher education distinct from later vocational schools under the {{ill|Senmon Gakkō Rei|ja|専門学校令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senmon Gakkō Rei|1=Senmon Gakkō Rei|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3258590}}. [[File:Koishikawa Annex.jpg|thumb|right|200px|The former Tokyo Medical School main building, relocated to the Koishikawa Botanical Garden, now the Koishikawa Annex of the University Museum.]]
== Overview == In May 1868 (Keiō 4), the {{ill|Meiji government|ja|明治政府|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji government|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 明治維新|1=Meiji restoration|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8707}} took over the {{ill|Medical School|ja|医学所|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Medical School|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|1=Medical School|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10950500}} of the former {{ill|Tokugawa shogunate|1=Tokugawa shogunate|2=ja|3=江戸幕府|ja|Tokugawa shogunate|1=Tokugawa shogunate|2=ja|3=江戸幕府|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa shogunate|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and reestablished it as the "Medical School." It later became part of the {{ill|University East Campus|ja|大学東校|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/University East Campus|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大学校 (1869年)|1=Daigakkō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11434269}} and was renamed Tokyo Medical School in 1874.
Together with the {{ill|Kaisei School|ja|開成学校|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaisei School|qq=}}, Tokyo Medical School formed the foundation of the {{ill|(Old) Tokyo University|ja|東京大学 (1877-1886)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/(Old) Tokyo University|1=Tokyo University|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11524659}} established in 1877. It is the predecessor of the [[:en:Graduate School of Medicine and Faculty of Medicine, University of Tokyo|University of Tokyo Graduate School of Medicine and Faculty of Medicine]].
== History ==
=== As the "Medical School" === In November 1861 (Bunkyū 1), the Tokugawa shogunate established the Western-style {{ill|Medical School|ja|医学所|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Medical School|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|1=Medical School|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10950500}}. In May 1868 (Keiō 4), the Meiji government took over this institution and renamed it the "Medical School" on {{ill|June 26 (old calendar)|ja|6月26日 (旧暦)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/June 26 (old calendar)|1=26th day of the 6th month in the Chinese calendar|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q839738}} (August 14, 1868, Gregorian calendar). In January 1869, William Willis, a physician attached to the British legation, began teaching there.
In February 1869, the Medical School merged with the "Large Hospital" to form the "Medical School and Hospital." On {{ill|July 8 (old calendar)|ja|7月8日 (旧暦)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/July 8 (old calendar)|1=8th day of the 7th month in the Chinese calendar|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q839706}} (August 15, 1869), it became part of the [[:en:Daigakkō|University]], under its jurisdiction as a "branch."
=== As "University East Campus" === {{Main|University East Campus}} On December 17, 1869 (January 18, 1870), the Medical School was renamed the "University East Campus" as part of the University restructuring. However, internal conflicts led to the closure of the {{ill|Main Campus|ja|大学本校|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Main Campus|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大学校 (1869年)|1=Daigakkō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11434269}} in August 1870, effectively rendering the University non-functional. The East Campus then operated independently, with new regulations implemented by October.
On {{ill|July 18 (old calendar)|ja|7月18日 (旧暦)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/July 18 (old calendar)|1=18th day of the 7th month in the Chinese calendar|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q839924}} (September 2, 1871), following the formal abolition of the University and the establishment of the [[:en:Ministry of Education, Science, Sports and Culture|Ministry of Education]], the East Campus was renamed "Tōkō" on {{ill|July 21 (old calendar)|ja|7月21日 (旧暦)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/July 21 (old calendar)|1=21st day of the 7th month in the Chinese calendar|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q839764}} (September 5, 1871). During this period, the transition from British to German medical education began.
=== As Tokyo Medical School === Following the introduction of the {{ill|Education System Order|ja|学制|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Education System Order|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Education System Order|WD=Q11448123}} in 1872, the school was renamed the "First University District Medical School" on {{ill|August 3 (old calendar)|ja|8月3日 (旧暦)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/August 3 (old calendar)|1=3rd day of the 8th month in the Chinese calendar|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q840073}} (September 5, 1872). In 1874, it was renamed Tokyo Medical School.
The school offered a two-year preparatory course and a five-year main course, primarily using German as the medium of instruction. In 1874, it introduced a simplified pharmaceutical training program (two years) and a short medical training course (three years), both taught in Japanese.
In 1877, Tokyo Medical School merged with the {{ill|Kaisei School|ja|東京開成学校|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaisei School|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 開成学校}} to form {{ill|(Old) Tokyo University|ja|東京大学 (1877-1886)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/(Old) Tokyo University|1=Tokyo University|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11524659}}. Initially, the Medical School maintained significant independence within the university, including its own head, equivalent to the university president.
=== Transition to German Medicine === Early in the Meiji era, the government favored British medicine due to its ties with Britain and its clinical focus. However, advocates like [[:en:Sagara Tomoyasu|Sagara Chian]] and {{ill|Iwasa Jun|ja|岩佐純|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwasa Jun|1=Iwasa Jun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q26243551}} promoted German medicine for its university-based research emphasis. Influential figures such as [[:en:Guido Verbeck|Guido Verbeck]] supported this shift, leading to the adoption of German medical education in 1871.
== Campus Locations and Legacy == The school initially occupied the former Medical School site in [[:en:Ueno|Ueno]] but later moved to {{ill|Hongo|ja|本郷|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hongo|1=Hongo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q19927172}}. Its 1876 campus, built in pseudo-Western architectural style, survived the {{ill|Great Kanto Earthquake|ja|関東大震災|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Great Kanto Earthquake|1=aftermath of 1923 Great Kantō earthquake|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3742341}} and was relocated to the {{ill|Koishikawa Botanical Garden|ja|小石川植物園|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Koishikawa Botanical Garden|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 東京大学大学院理学系研究科附属植物園|1=Koishikawa Botanical Garden|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1064892}}. It now serves as the oldest building associated with the University of Tokyo.
== Related Literature == ; Books * Akira Kajita, ''History of Medicine'', Kodansha Academic Library, 2003 ISBN 4061596144 * Masao Terasaki, ''History of the University of Tokyo: Pioneer of the University System'', Kodansha Academic Library, 2007 ISBN 9784061597990
== Related Articles == * [[:en:Erwin Bälz|Erwin von Baelz]] * {{ill|Keio Medical School|ja|慶應義塾医学所|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Keio Medical School|1=Keio Medical School|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11495139}} * {{ill|Nagasaki Medical School|ja|長崎医学校|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagasaki Medical School|1=Nagasaki Medical School|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11652415}}
{{Tokyo University Predecessors}} {{school-stub}}

In 1874, it was renamed Tokyo Medical School.
The school offered a two-year preparatory course and a five-year main course, primarily using German as the medium of instruction. In 1874, it introduced a simplified pharmaceutical training program (two years) and a short medical training course (three years), both taught in Japanese.
In 1877, Tokyo Medical School merged with the {{ill|Kaisei School|ja|東京開成学校|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaisei School|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 開成学校}} to form {{ill|(Old) Tokyo University|ja|東京大学 (1877-1886)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/(Old) Tokyo University|1=Tokyo University|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11524659}}. Initially, the Medical School maintained significant independence within the university, including its own head, equivalent to the university president.
=== Transition to German Medicine === Early in the Meiji era, the government favored British medicine due to its ties with Britain and its clinical focus. However, advocates like [[:en:Sagara Tomoyasu|Sagara Chian]] and {{ill|Iwasa Jun|ja|岩佐純|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwasa Jun|1=Iwasa Jun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q26243551}} promoted German medicine for its university-based research emphasis. Influential figures such as [[:en:Guido Verbeck|Guido Verbeck]] supported this shift, leading to the adoption of German medical education in 1871.
== Campus Locations and Legacy == The school initially occupied the former Medical School site in [[:en:Ueno|Ueno]] but later moved to {{ill|Hongo|ja|本郷|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hongo|1=Hongo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q19927172}}. Its 1876 campus, built in pseudo-Western architectural style, survived the {{ill|Great Kanto Earthquake|ja|関東大震災|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Great Kanto Earthquake|1=aftermath of 1923 Great Kantō earthquake|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3742341}} and was relocated to the {{ill|Koishikawa Botanical Garden|ja|小石川植物園|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Koishikawa Botanical Garden|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 東京大学大学院理学系研究科附属植物園|1=Koishikawa Botanical Garden|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1064892}}. It now serves as the oldest building associated with the University of Tokyo.
== Related Literature == ; Books * Akira Kajita, ''History of Medicine'', Kodansha Academic Library, 2003 ISBN 4061596144 * Masao Terasaki, ''History of the University of Tokyo: Pioneer of the University System'', Kodansha Academic Library, 2007 ISBN 9784061597990
== Related Articles == * [[:en:Erwin Bälz|Erwin von Baelz]] * {{ill|Keio Medical School|ja|慶應義塾医学所|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Keio Medical School|1=Keio Medical School|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11495139}} * {{ill|Nagasaki Medical School|ja|長崎医学校|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagasaki Medical School|1=Nagasaki Medical School|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11652415}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Tokyo Medical School]][[ja:東京医学校]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11524573]] Wikidata name: Tokyo Medical School [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:00, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Tokyo Medical School''' (東京医学校) was an early Meiji-era government medical education institution located in Tokyo Prefecture. It was one of the "specialized schools" of its time, representing higher education distinct from later vocational schools under the {{ill|Senmon Gakkō Rei|ja|専門学校令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senmon Gakkō Rei|1=Senmon Gakkō Rei|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3258590}}. [[File:Koishikawa Annex.jpg|thumb|right|200px|The former Tokyo Medical School main building, relocated to the Koishikawa Botanical Garden, now the Koishikawa Annex of the University Museum.]]
== Overview == In May 1868 (Keiō 4), the {{ill|Meiji government|ja|明治政府|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji government|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 明治維新|1=Meiji restoration|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8707}} took over the {{ill|Medical School|ja|医学所|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Medical School|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|1=Medical School|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10950500}} of the former {{ill|Tokugawa shogunate|1=Tokugawa shogunate|2=ja|3=江戸幕府|ja|Tokugawa shogunate|1=Tokugawa shogunate|2=ja|3=江戸幕府|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa shogunate|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and reestablished it as the "Medical School." It later became part of the {{ill|University East Campus|ja|大学東校|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/University East Campus|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大学校 (1869年)|1=Daigakkō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11434269}} and was renamed Tokyo Medical School in 1874.
Together with the {{ill|Kaisei School|ja|開成学校|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaisei School|qq=}}, Tokyo Medical School formed the foundation of the {{ill|(Old) Tokyo University|ja|東京大学 (1877-1886)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/(Old) Tokyo University|1=Tokyo University|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11524659}} established in 1877. It is the predecessor of the [[:en:Graduate School of Medicine and Faculty of Medicine, University of Tokyo|University of Tokyo Graduate School of Medicine and Faculty of Medicine]].
== History ==
=== As the "Medical School" === In November 1861 (Bunkyū 1), the Tokugawa shogunate established the Western-style {{ill|Medical School|ja|医学所|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Medical School|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|1=Medical School|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10950500}}. In May 1868 (Keiō 4), the Meiji government took over this institution and renamed it the "Medical School" on {{ill|June 26 (old calendar)|ja|6月26日 (旧暦)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/June 26 (old calendar)|1=26th day of the 6th month in the Chinese calendar|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q839738}} (August 14, 1868, Gregorian calendar). In January 1869, William Willis, a physician attached to the British legation, began teaching there.
In February 1869, the Medical School merged with the "Large Hospital" to form the "Medical School and Hospital." On {{ill|July 8 (old calendar)|ja|7月8日 (旧暦)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/July 8 (old calendar)|1=8th day of the 7th month in the Chinese calendar|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q839706}} (August 15, 1869), it became part of the [[:en:Daigakkō|University]], under its jurisdiction as a "branch."
=== As "University East Campus" === {{Main|University East Campus}} On December 17, 1869 (January 18, 1870), the Medical School was renamed the "University East Campus" as part of the University restructuring. However, internal conflicts led to the closure of the {{ill|Main Campus|ja|大学本校|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Main Campus|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大学校 (1869年)|1=Daigakkō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11434269}} in August 1870, effectively rendering the University non-functional. The East Campus then operated independently, with new regulations implemented by October.
On {{ill|July 18 (old calendar)|ja|7月18日 (旧暦)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/July 18 (old calendar)|1=18th day of the 7th month in the Chinese calendar|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q839924}} (September 2, 1871), following the formal abolition of the University and the establishment of the [[:en:Ministry of Education, Science, Sports and Culture|Ministry of Education]], the East Campus was renamed "Tōkō" on {{ill|July 21 (old calendar)|ja|7月21日 (旧暦)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/July 21 (old calendar)|1=21st day of the 7th month in the Chinese calendar|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q839764}} (September 5, 1871). During this period, the transition from British to German medical education began.
=== As Tokyo Medical School === Following the introduction of the {{ill|Education System Order|ja|学制|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Education System Order|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Education System Order|WD=Q11448123}} in 1872, the school was renamed the "First University District Medical School" on {{ill|August 3 (old calendar)|ja|8月3日 (旧暦)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/August 3 (old calendar)|1=3rd day of the 8th month in the Chinese calendar|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q840073}} (September 5, 1872). In 1874, it was renamed Tokyo Medical School.
The school offered a two-year preparatory course and a five-year main course, primarily using German as the medium of instruction. In 1874, it introduced a simplified pharmaceutical training program (two years) and a short medical training course (three years), both taught in Japanese.
In 1877, Tokyo Medical School merged with the {{ill|Kaisei School|ja|東京開成学校|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaisei School|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 開成学校}} to form {{ill|(Old) Tokyo University|ja|東京大学 (1877-1886)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/(Old) Tokyo University|1=Tokyo University|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11524659}}. Initially, the Medical School maintained significant independence within the university, including its own head, equivalent to the university president.
=== Transition to German Medicine === Early in the Meiji era, the government favored British medicine due to its ties with Britain and its clinical focus. However, advocates like [[:en:Sagara Tomoyasu|Sagara Chian]] and {{ill|Iwasa Jun|ja|岩佐純|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwasa Jun|1=Iwasa Jun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q26243551}} promoted German medicine for its university-based research emphasis. Influential figures such as [[:en:Guido Verbeck|Guido Verbeck]] supported this shift, leading to the adoption of German medical education in 1871.
== Campus Locations and Legacy == The school initially occupied the former Medical School site in [[:en:Ueno|Ueno]] but later moved to {{ill|Hongo|ja|本郷|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hongo|1=Hongo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q19927172}}. Its 1876 campus, built in pseudo-Western architectural style, survived the {{ill|Great Kanto Earthquake|ja|関東大震災|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Great Kanto Earthquake|1=aftermath of 1923 Great Kantō earthquake|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3742341}} and was relocated to the {{ill|Koishikawa Botanical Garden|ja|小石川植物園|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Koishikawa Botanical Garden|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 東京大学大学院理学系研究科附属植物園|1=Koishikawa Botanical Garden|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1064892}}. It now serves as the oldest building associated with the University of Tokyo.
== Related Literature == ; Books * Akira Kajita, ''History of Medicine'', Kodansha Academic Library, 2003 ISBN 4061596144 * Masao Terasaki, ''History of the University of Tokyo: Pioneer of the University System'', Kodansha Academic Library, 2007 ISBN 9784061597990
== Related Articles == * [[:en:Erwin Bälz|Erwin von Baelz]] * {{ill|Keio Medical School|ja|慶應義塾医学所|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Keio Medical School|1=Keio Medical School|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11495139}} * {{ill|Nagasaki Medical School|ja|長崎医学校|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagasaki Medical School|1=Nagasaki Medical School|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11652415}}

It is the predecessor of the [[:en:Graduate School of Medicine and Faculty of Medicine, University of Tokyo|University of Tokyo Graduate School of Medicine and Faculty of Medicine]].

=== As the "Medical School" === In November 1861 (Bunkyū 1), the Tokugawa shogunate established the Western-style {{ill|Medical School|ja|医学所|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Medical School|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|1=Medical School|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10950500}}. In May 1868 (Keiō 4), the Meiji government took over this institution and renamed it the "Medical School" on {{ill|June 26 (old calendar)|ja|6月26日 (旧暦)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/June 26 (old calendar)|1=26th day of the 6th month in the Chinese calendar|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q839738}} (August 14, 1868, Gregorian calendar). In January 1869, William Willis, a physician attached to the British legation, began teaching there.
In February 1869, the Medical School merged with the "Large Hospital" to form the "Medical School and Hospital." On {{ill|July 8 (old calendar)|ja|7月8日 (旧暦)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/July 8 (old calendar)|1=8th day of the 7th month in the Chinese calendar|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q839706}} (August 15, 1869), it became part of the [[:en:Daigakkō|University]], under its jurisdiction as a "branch."
=== As "University East Campus" === {{Main|University East Campus}} On December 17, 1869 (January 18, 1870), the Medical School was renamed the "University East Campus" as part of the University restructuring. However, internal conflicts led to the closure of the {{ill|Main Campus|ja|大学本校|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Main Campus|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大学校 (1869年)|1=Daigakkō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11434269}} in August 1870, effectively rendering the University non-functional. The East Campus then operated independently, with new regulations implemented by October.
On {{ill|July 18 (old calendar)|ja|7月18日 (旧暦)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/July 18 (old calendar)|1=18th day of the 7th month in the Chinese calendar|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q839924}} (September 2, 1871), following the formal abolition of the University and the establishment of the [[:en:Ministry of Education, Science, Sports and Culture|Ministry of Education]], the East Campus was renamed "Tōkō" on {{ill|July 21 (old calendar)|ja|7月21日 (旧暦)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/July 21 (old calendar)|1=21st day of the 7th month in the Chinese calendar|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q839764}} (September 5, 1871). During this period, the transition from British to German medical education began.
=== As Tokyo Medical School === Following the introduction of the {{ill|Education System Order|ja|学制|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Education System Order|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Education System Order|WD=Q11448123}} in 1872, the school was renamed the "First University District Medical School" on {{ill|August 3 (old calendar)|ja|8月3日 (旧暦)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/August 3 (old calendar)|1=3rd day of the 8th month in the Chinese calendar|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q840073}} (September 5, 1872). In 1874, it was renamed Tokyo Medical School.
The school offered a two-year preparatory course and a five-year main course, primarily using German as the medium of instruction. In 1874, it introduced a simplified pharmaceutical training program (two years) and a short medical training course (three years), both taught in Japanese.
In 1877, Tokyo Medical School merged with the {{ill|Kaisei School|ja|東京開成学校|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaisei School|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 開成学校}} to form {{ill|(Old) Tokyo University|ja|東京大学 (1877-1886)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/(Old) Tokyo University|1=Tokyo University|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11524659}}. Initially, the Medical School maintained significant independence within the university, including its own head, equivalent to the university president.
=== Transition to German Medicine === Early in the Meiji era, the government favored British medicine due to its ties with Britain and its clinical focus. However, advocates like [[:en:Sagara Tomoyasu|Sagara Chian]] and {{ill|Iwasa Jun|ja|岩佐純|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwasa Jun|1=Iwasa Jun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q26243551}} promoted German medicine for its university-based research emphasis. Influential figures such as [[:en:Guido Verbeck|Guido Verbeck]] supported this shift, leading to the adoption of German medical education in 1871.
== Campus Locations and Legacy == The school initially occupied the former Medical School site in [[:en:Ueno|Ueno]] but later moved to {{ill|Hongo|ja|本郷|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hongo|1=Hongo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q19927172}}. Its 1876 campus, built in pseudo-Western architectural style, survived the {{ill|Great Kanto Earthquake|ja|関東大震災|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Great Kanto Earthquake|1=aftermath of 1923 Great Kantō earthquake|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3742341}} and was relocated to the {{ill|Koishikawa Botanical Garden|ja|小石川植物園|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Koishikawa Botanical Garden|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 東京大学大学院理学系研究科附属植物園|1=Koishikawa Botanical Garden|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1064892}}. It now serves as the oldest building associated with the University of Tokyo.

[[File:Koishikawa Annex.jpg|thumb|right|200px|The former Tokyo Medical School main building, relocated to the Koishikawa Botanical Garden, now the Koishikawa Annex of the University Museum.]]

revision.comment: Add page with conflicting Wikidata QIDs
revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Tokyo Medical School]][[ja:東京医学校]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Tokyo Medical School]][[ja:東京医学校]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Tokyo Medical School]][[ja:東京医学校]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11524573]] Wikidata name: Tokyo Medical School [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:00, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Tokyo Medical School''' (東京医学校) was an early Meiji-era government medical education institution located in Tokyo Prefecture. It was one of the "specialized schools" of its time, representing higher education distinct from later vocational schools under the {{ill|Senmon Gakkō Rei|ja|専門学校令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senmon Gakkō Rei|1=Senmon Gakkō Rei|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3258590}}. [[File:Koishikawa Annex.jpg|thumb|right|200px|The former Tokyo Medical School main building, relocated to the Koishikawa Botanical Garden, now the Koishikawa Annex of the University Museum.]]
== Overview == In May 1868 (Keiō 4), the {{ill|Meiji government|ja|明治政府|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji government|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 明治維新|1=Meiji restoration|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8707}} took over the {{ill|Medical School|ja|医学所|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Medical School|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|1=Medical School|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10950500}} of the former {{ill|Tokugawa shogunate|1=Tokugawa shogunate|2=ja|3=江戸幕府|ja|Tokugawa shogunate|1=Tokugawa shogunate|2=ja|3=江戸幕府|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa shogunate|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and reestablished it as the "Medical School." It later became part of the {{ill|University East Campus|ja|大学東校|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/University East Campus|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大学校 (1869年)|1=Daigakkō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11434269}} and was renamed Tokyo Medical School in 1874.
Together with the {{ill|Kaisei School|ja|開成学校|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaisei School|qq=}}, Tokyo Medical School formed the foundation of the {{ill|(Old) Tokyo University|ja|東京大学 (1877-1886)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/(Old) Tokyo University|1=Tokyo University|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11524659}} established in 1877. It is the predecessor of the [[:en:Graduate School of Medicine and Faculty of Medicine, University of Tokyo|University of Tokyo Graduate School of Medicine and Faculty of Medicine]].
== History ==
=== As the "Medical School" === In November 1861 (Bunkyū 1), the Tokugawa shogunate established the Western-style {{ill|Medical School|ja|医学所|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Medical School|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|1=Medical School|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10950500}}. In May 1868 (Keiō 4), the Meiji government took over this institution and renamed it the "Medical School" on {{ill|June 26 (old calendar)|ja|6月26日 (旧暦)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/June 26 (old calendar)|1=26th day of the 6th month in the Chinese calendar|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q839738}} (August 14, 1868, Gregorian calendar). In January 1869, William Willis, a physician attached to the British legation, began teaching there.
In February 1869, the Medical School merged with the "Large Hospital" to form the "Medical School and Hospital." On {{ill|July 8 (old calendar)|ja|7月8日 (旧暦)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/July 8 (old calendar)|1=8th day of the 7th month in the Chinese calendar|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q839706}} (August 15, 1869), it became part of the [[:en:Daigakkō|University]], under its jurisdiction as a "branch."
=== As "University East Campus" === {{Main|University East Campus}} On December 17, 1869 (January 18, 1870), the Medical School was renamed the "University East Campus" as part of the University restructuring. However, internal conflicts led to the closure of the {{ill|Main Campus|ja|大学本校|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Main Campus|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大学校 (1869年)|1=Daigakkō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11434269}} in August 1870, effectively rendering the University non-functional. The East Campus then operated independently, with new regulations implemented by October.
On {{ill|July 18 (old calendar)|ja|7月18日 (旧暦)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/July 18 (old calendar)|1=18th day of the 7th month in the Chinese calendar|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q839924}} (September 2, 1871), following the formal abolition of the University and the establishment of the [[:en:Ministry of Education, Science, Sports and Culture|Ministry of Education]], the East Campus was renamed "Tōkō" on {{ill|July 21 (old calendar)|ja|7月21日 (旧暦)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/July 21 (old calendar)|1=21st day of the 7th month in the Chinese calendar|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q839764}} (September 5, 1871). During this period, the transition from British to German medical education began.
=== As Tokyo Medical School === Following the introduction of the {{ill|Education System Order|ja|学制|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Education System Order|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Education System Order|WD=Q11448123}} in 1872, the school was renamed the "First University District Medical School" on {{ill|August 3 (old calendar)|ja|8月3日 (旧暦)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/August 3 (old calendar)|1=3rd day of the 8th month in the Chinese calendar|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q840073}} (September 5, 1872). In 1874, it was renamed Tokyo Medical School.
The school offered a two-year preparatory course and a five-year main course, primarily using German as the medium of instruction. In 1874, it introduced a simplified pharmaceutical training program (two years) and a short medical training course (three years), both taught in Japanese.
In 1877, Tokyo Medical School merged with the {{ill|Kaisei School|ja|東京開成学校|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaisei School|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 開成学校}} to form {{ill|(Old) Tokyo University|ja|東京大学 (1877-1886)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/(Old) Tokyo University|1=Tokyo University|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11524659}}. Initially, the Medical School maintained significant independence within the university, including its own head, equivalent to the university president.
=== Transition to German Medicine === Early in the Meiji era, the government favored British medicine due to its ties with Britain and its clinical focus. However, advocates like [[:en:Sagara Tomoyasu|Sagara Chian]] and {{ill|Iwasa Jun|ja|岩佐純|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwasa Jun|1=Iwasa Jun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q26243551}} promoted German medicine for its university-based research emphasis. Influential figures such as [[:en:Guido Verbeck|Guido Verbeck]] supported this shift, leading to the adoption of German medical education in 1871.
== Campus Locations and Legacy == The school initially occupied the former Medical School site in [[:en:Ueno|Ueno]] but later moved to {{ill|Hongo|ja|本郷|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hongo|1=Hongo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q19927172}}. Its 1876 campus, built in pseudo-Western architectural style, survived the {{ill|Great Kanto Earthquake|ja|関東大震災|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Great Kanto Earthquake|1=aftermath of 1923 Great Kantō earthquake|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3742341}} and was relocated to the {{ill|Koishikawa Botanical Garden|ja|小石川植物園|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Koishikawa Botanical Garden|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 東京大学大学院理学系研究科附属植物園|1=Koishikawa Botanical Garden|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1064892}}. It now serves as the oldest building associated with the University of Tokyo.
== Related Literature == ; Books * Akira Kajita, ''History of Medicine'', Kodansha Academic Library, 2003 ISBN 4061596144 * Masao Terasaki, ''History of the University of Tokyo: Pioneer of the University System'', Kodansha Academic Library, 2007 ISBN 9784061597990
== Related Articles == * [[:en:Erwin Bälz|Erwin von Baelz]] * {{ill|Keio Medical School|ja|慶應義塾医学所|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Keio Medical School|1=Keio Medical School|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11495139}} * {{ill|Nagasaki Medical School|ja|長崎医学校|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagasaki Medical School|1=Nagasaki Medical School|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11652415}}
{{Tokyo University Predecessors}} {{school-stub}}

[[Category:Medical schools in Japan]]

In 1874, it was renamed Tokyo Medical School.
The school offered a two-year preparatory course and a five-year main course, primarily using German as the medium of instruction. In 1874, it introduced a simplified pharmaceutical training program (two years) and a short medical training course (three years), both taught in Japanese.
In 1877, Tokyo Medical School merged with the {{ill|Kaisei School|ja|東京開成学校|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaisei School|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 開成学校}} to form {{ill|(Old) Tokyo University|ja|東京大学 (1877-1886)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/(Old) Tokyo University|1=Tokyo University|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11524659}}. Initially, the Medical School maintained significant independence within the university, including its own head, equivalent to the university president.
=== Transition to German Medicine === Early in the Meiji era, the government favored British medicine due to its ties with Britain and its clinical focus. However, advocates like [[:en:Sagara Tomoyasu|Sagara Chian]] and {{ill|Iwasa Jun|ja|岩佐純|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwasa Jun|1=Iwasa Jun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q26243551}} promoted German medicine for its university-based research emphasis. Influential figures such as [[:en:Guido Verbeck|Guido Verbeck]] supported this shift, leading to the adoption of German medical education in 1871.
== Campus Locations and Legacy == The school initially occupied the former Medical School site in [[:en:Ueno|Ueno]] but later moved to {{ill|Hongo|ja|本郷|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hongo|1=Hongo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q19927172}}. Its 1876 campus, built in pseudo-Western architectural style, survived the {{ill|Great Kanto Earthquake|ja|関東大震災|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Great Kanto Earthquake|1=aftermath of 1923 Great Kantō earthquake|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3742341}} and was relocated to the {{ill|Koishikawa Botanical Garden|ja|小石川植物園|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Koishikawa Botanical Garden|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 東京大学大学院理学系研究科附属植物園|1=Koishikawa Botanical Garden|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1064892}}. It now serves as the oldest building associated with the University of Tokyo.

{{wikidata link|Q11524573}} [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:Education of the Meiji Period]] [[Category:Former Educational Institutions of Tokyo]] [[Category:Former Japanese Medical Education Institutions]] [[Category:Germany–Japan relations]] [[Category:History of the University of Tokyo]] [[Category:Medical Schools]] [[Category:Medical schools in Japan]] [[Category:pages with conflicting qids]] [[Category:Western Studies (Yōgaku)]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Tokyo Medical School]][[ja:東京医学校]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Tokyo Medical School]][[ja:東京医学校]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Tokyo Medical School]][[ja:東京医学校]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11524573]] Wikidata name: Tokyo Medical School [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:00, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Tokyo Medical School''' (東京医学校) was an early Meiji-era government medical education institution located in Tokyo Prefecture. It was one of the "specialized schools" of its time, representing higher education distinct from later vocational schools under the {{ill|Senmon Gakkō Rei|ja|専門学校令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senmon Gakkō Rei|1=Senmon Gakkō Rei|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3258590}}. [[File:Koishikawa Annex.jpg|thumb|right|200px|The former Tokyo Medical School main building, relocated to the Koishikawa Botanical Garden, now the Koishikawa Annex of the University Museum.]]
== Overview == In May 1868 (Keiō 4), the {{ill|Meiji government|ja|明治政府|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji government|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 明治維新|1=Meiji restoration|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8707}} took over the {{ill|Medical School|ja|医学所|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Medical School|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|1=Medical School|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10950500}} of the former {{ill|Tokugawa shogunate|1=Tokugawa shogunate|2=ja|3=江戸幕府|ja|Tokugawa shogunate|1=Tokugawa shogunate|2=ja|3=江戸幕府|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa shogunate|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and reestablished it as the "Medical School." It later became part of the {{ill|University East Campus|ja|大学東校|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/University East Campus|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大学校 (1869年)|1=Daigakkō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11434269}} and was renamed Tokyo Medical School in 1874.
Together with the {{ill|Kaisei School|ja|開成学校|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaisei School|qq=}}, Tokyo Medical School formed the foundation of the {{ill|(Old) Tokyo University|ja|東京大学 (1877-1886)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/(Old) Tokyo University|1=Tokyo University|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11524659}} established in 1877. It is the predecessor of the [[:en:Graduate School of Medicine and Faculty of Medicine, University of Tokyo|University of Tokyo Graduate School of Medicine and Faculty of Medicine]].
== History ==
=== As the "Medical School" === In November 1861 (Bunkyū 1), the Tokugawa shogunate established the Western-style {{ill|Medical School|ja|医学所|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Medical School|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|1=Medical School|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10950500}}. In May 1868 (Keiō 4), the Meiji government took over this institution and renamed it the "Medical School" on {{ill|June 26 (old calendar)|ja|6月26日 (旧暦)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/June 26 (old calendar)|1=26th day of the 6th month in the Chinese calendar|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q839738}} (August 14, 1868, Gregorian calendar). In January 1869, William Willis, a physician attached to the British legation, began teaching there.
In February 1869, the Medical School merged with the "Large Hospital" to form the "Medical School and Hospital." On {{ill|July 8 (old calendar)|ja|7月8日 (旧暦)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/July 8 (old calendar)|1=8th day of the 7th month in the Chinese calendar|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q839706}} (August 15, 1869), it became part of the [[:en:Daigakkō|University]], under its jurisdiction as a "branch."
=== As "University East Campus" === {{Main|University East Campus}} On December 17, 1869 (January 18, 1870), the Medical School was renamed the "University East Campus" as part of the University restructuring. However, internal conflicts led to the closure of the {{ill|Main Campus|ja|大学本校|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Main Campus|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大学校 (1869年)|1=Daigakkō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11434269}} in August 1870, effectively rendering the University non-functional. The East Campus then operated independently, with new regulations implemented by October.
On {{ill|July 18 (old calendar)|ja|7月18日 (旧暦)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/July 18 (old calendar)|1=18th day of the 7th month in the Chinese calendar|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q839924}} (September 2, 1871), following the formal abolition of the University and the establishment of the [[:en:Ministry of Education, Science, Sports and Culture|Ministry of Education]], the East Campus was renamed "Tōkō" on {{ill|July 21 (old calendar)|ja|7月21日 (旧暦)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/July 21 (old calendar)|1=21st day of the 7th month in the Chinese calendar|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q839764}} (September 5, 1871). During this period, the transition from British to German medical education began.
=== As Tokyo Medical School === Following the introduction of the {{ill|Education System Order|ja|学制|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Education System Order|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Education System Order|WD=Q11448123}} in 1872, the school was renamed the "First University District Medical School" on {{ill|August 3 (old calendar)|ja|8月3日 (旧暦)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/August 3 (old calendar)|1=3rd day of the 8th month in the Chinese calendar|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q840073}} (September 5, 1872). In 1874, it was renamed Tokyo Medical School.
The school offered a two-year preparatory course and a five-year main course, primarily using German as the medium of instruction. In 1874, it introduced a simplified pharmaceutical training program (two years) and a short medical training course (three years), both taught in Japanese.
In 1877, Tokyo Medical School merged with the {{ill|Kaisei School|ja|東京開成学校|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaisei School|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 開成学校}} to form {{ill|(Old) Tokyo University|ja|東京大学 (1877-1886)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/(Old) Tokyo University|1=Tokyo University|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11524659}}. Initially, the Medical School maintained significant independence within the university, including its own head, equivalent to the university president.
=== Transition to German Medicine === Early in the Meiji era, the government favored British medicine due to its ties with Britain and its clinical focus. However, advocates like [[:en:Sagara Tomoyasu|Sagara Chian]] and {{ill|Iwasa Jun|ja|岩佐純|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwasa Jun|1=Iwasa Jun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q26243551}} promoted German medicine for its university-based research emphasis. Influential figures such as [[:en:Guido Verbeck|Guido Verbeck]] supported this shift, leading to the adoption of German medical education in 1871.
== Campus Locations and Legacy == The school initially occupied the former Medical School site in [[:en:Ueno|Ueno]] but later moved to {{ill|Hongo|ja|本郷|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hongo|1=Hongo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q19927172}}. Its 1876 campus, built in pseudo-Western architectural style, survived the {{ill|Great Kanto Earthquake|ja|関東大震災|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Great Kanto Earthquake|1=aftermath of 1923 Great Kantō earthquake|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3742341}} and was relocated to the {{ill|Koishikawa Botanical Garden|ja|小石川植物園|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Koishikawa Botanical Garden|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 東京大学大学院理学系研究科附属植物園|1=Koishikawa Botanical Garden|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1064892}}. It now serves as the oldest building associated with the University of Tokyo.
